text:
There is a particular kind of grief that arrives with the last page, and it is unlike any other. You have spent hours or days or weeks inside another world, breathing its air, hearing its voices, and then suddenly the text thins, the white space below the final paragraph grows and grows, and you are being expelled.

The last page is where the author must make a choice that reveals everything about what they believe a story is for. Some writers close the door firmly, resolving the tension and leaving you with the satisfying click of a lock turning. Others leave it ajar, trusting that the reader can carry what remains into their own life and make something of it there. And then there are those rare writers who seem to fling the door entirely off its hinges, so that when you finish you are not sure you have finished at all, only that the book has stopped while the story continues somewhere you cannot follow.

The physical act of closing a book carries a strange ceremony. The covers come together. The spine resists slightly, as if reluctant. Whatever world existed in those pages collapses into a rectangle of paper and ink no larger than a human hand. It becomes an object again, and you are returned to a room, to a chair, to the particular quality of light at whatever hour it happens to be.

What lingers after a last page is almost never the plot. It is a quality of feeling, a texture, the way a certain sentence moved. You carry it around for a few days like something fragile, and then life crowds in and it settles somewhere deeper, joining the sediment of every other book you have loved, all of it quietly shaping the way you see.

The last page is also a beginning, of course. That is the oldest thing to say about it, but it remains true. The book is finished. The reading has just started.
